# Project 1: Linear Regression Guidelines {#sec-guidelines-linear}

{{< include ../_common.qmd >}}

This chapter walks you through the seven required parts of the linear regression project. For each part, you will find the expectations, page limits, and advice to help you produce a strong final product. Read this chapter carefully before you begin writing.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Review Before You Draft
For the statistical ideas, review [Simple Linear Regression](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/01-simple-linear-regression.html), [Continuous Predictors](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/04-continuous-predictors.html), [Interactions](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/05-interactions.html), [Nonlinear Relationships](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/06-nonlinear-relationships.html), [Model Building](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/07-model-building.html), and [Model Diagnostics](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/08-model-diagnostics.html). For the R workflow, review [Lab 05](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab05-multiple-regression.html), [Lab 06](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab06-interactions.html), [Lab 07](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab07-model-building.html), and [Lab 08](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab08-diagnostics.html).
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Overall Page Limits
The main text of Project 1 (Parts 1--6) should not exceed approximately **6 pages** of single-spaced text. The section budgets below sum to 6 pages of main text. Parts 4--5 allow up to **5 additional pages** of tables, figures, and essential diagnostic displays. Appendix and reproducibility materials are separate. Quality and clarity matter more than length.
:::


## Part 1: Background and Research Questions {#sec-linear-part1}

*Maximum length: 1 page*

Begin your project by motivating the topic and stating your research questions. The goal is to convince the reader that the questions you are asking are worth investigating and that a regression analysis is the right tool to address them.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 1
- Provide a brief paragraph motivating **why** the topic matters.
- Formulate **2--3 research questions** that you will address with linear regression. This 2--3 RQ pattern is for Project 1; Project 2 has its own binary-outcome RQ policy.
- **Number** your research questions (RQ1, RQ2, RQ3). You will refer back to these numbers throughout the project.
- Each research question should involve a clear outcome variable and one or more predictor variables.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Research Question Template
A well-formed research question for a regression project often follows this pattern:

> "Is there a relationship between [outcome Y] and [predictor X], after controlling for [covariates]?"

or

> "Is there a difference in [outcome Y] between [groups defined by a categorical predictor] after controlling for [covariates]?"

These templates help ensure your questions are answerable with the methods you will use.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 1
- **Describing the data here.** Save all data and variable descriptions for Part 2. Part 1 should focus on the *why*, not the *what*.
- **Stating null hypotheses.** This project does not require formal null and alternative hypotheses. Focus on substantive research questions.
- **Asking vague questions.** Avoid questions like "What predicts Y?" without specifying which predictors you plan to examine.
:::


## Part 2: Methods --- Subjects and Variables {#sec-linear-part2}

*Maximum length: 1 page*

This section describes the data you will analyze. The reader should finish Part 2 knowing exactly what dataset you are using, who the subjects are, and how every variable is measured and coded.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 2
- Identify the **data source** and briefly describe the study design (e.g., survey, observational study).
- Describe the **subjects**: who they are, how they were sampled, the starting sample size, any exclusions or restrictions, and the final analytic sample size.
- Summarize missingness on the variables used in your planned model sequence.
- For **each variable** in your analysis, provide:
  - The variable name (as it appears in the dataset)
  - Whether it is **continuous** or **categorical**
  - The measurement scale or unit (e.g., years, dollars, Likert scale 1--5)
  - For categorical variables: list **all categories** and specify which category serves as the **reference group**
- Define any recodes, transformations, centered variables, or dummy variables you create.
- If you create dummy variables from a categorical predictor, define each dummy variable explicitly (e.g., $D_1 = 1$ if female, 0 otherwise).
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Organizing Variable Descriptions
A concise table can help the reader quickly grasp your variables:

| Variable | Role | Type | Coding / Description |
|----------|------|------|----------------------|
| `earnings` | Outcome | Continuous | Monthly earnings in USD |
| `education` | Predictor | Continuous | Years of formal education |
| `gender` | Predictor | Categorical | Male (ref), Female |
| `region` | Predictor | Categorical | Urban (ref), Suburban, Rural |

Follow the table with a sentence or two defining the analytic sample and any dummy variables for categorical predictors.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 2
- **Listing categories without defining the reference group.** For every categorical variable, you must state which category is the reference. Without this, the reader cannot interpret your regression coefficients.
- **Omitting the coding scheme for dummy variables.** If `region` has three categories, you need two dummy variables. Define them explicitly.
- **Including variables you do not use in the analysis.** Every variable described here should appear in at least one of your models.
:::


## Part 3: Methods --- Statistical Analysis {#sec-linear-part3}

*Maximum length: half a page*

This short section tells the reader exactly which regression model(s) you will fit and connects each model to a specific research question.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 3
- State the regression model(s) you will estimate. You may write them in equation form or in words.
- **Link each model to specific research questions.** For example: "To address RQ1 and RQ2, we fit the following model: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \beta_3 X_3 + \varepsilon$."
- If you fit more than one model (e.g., a reduced model and a full model), present a **planned model sequence** and explain the purpose of each model.
- State that all nested models will be fit on the same complete-case analysis dataset.
- If you include an interaction or nonlinear term, explain which research question motivates it before you fit it.
- State the significance level you will use (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Planned Model Sequence and R Workflow
A clear analysis plan often has a small table like this:

| Model | Predictors added | RQ addressed | Planned comparison |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model 1 | Key predictor + basic controls | RQ1 | Baseline model |
| Model 2 | Add categorical predictor block | RQ2 | Compare with Model 1 |
| Model 3 | Add interaction term, if justified | RQ3 | Compare with Model 2 |

In R, use one analysis dataset such as `analysis_df`, fit models with `lm()`, inspect results with `model_params()` and `model_fit()`, compare planned models with `compare_models()` or `anova()`, and build final report tables with `gtsummary::tbl_regression()`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Connecting Models to Research Questions
A clear way to organize this section:

- "To address **RQ1**, we regressed monthly earnings on years of education, gender, and region."
- "To address **RQ2**, we added an interaction term between education and gender to examine whether the relationship between education and earnings differs by gender."

This makes it easy for the reader (and the grader) to follow your analytic logic.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 3
- **Describing the model without linking it to a research question.** Every model must have a purpose tied to your RQs.
- **Changing samples across nested models.** If Model 1 and Model 2 use different rows, the comparison is not clean.
- **Adding terms just to search for significance.** Interactions, transformations, and polynomial terms should be motivated by your research questions, theory, or clear diagnostic evidence.
- **Forgetting to mention the significance level.** A simple sentence such as "All tests were conducted at the $\alpha = 0.05$ level" is sufficient.
:::


## Part 4: Results --- Descriptive Analyses {#sec-linear-part4}

*Maximum length: 1 page of text + up to 3 pages of tables and figures*

Before jumping into regression results, you need to describe your data. Descriptive statistics and visualizations help you (and the reader) understand the distributions, spot potential problems, and set the stage for the regression analysis.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 4
- Report the **final analytic sample size** and describe any missing data.
- For **continuous variables**: report the mean, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum.
- For **categorical variables**: report frequency counts and percentages.
- Use the same complete-case analysis dataset that will be used for the largest planned regression model.
- A `gtsummary::tbl_summary()` table is a good default: mean/SD/min/max for continuous variables and n/% for categorical variables.
- Include the most relevant **graphs** in the report, and place extras in the appendix if useful:
  - A **histogram** of the outcome variable
  - **Scatterplots** of the outcome vs. key continuous predictors
  - **Boxplots** of the outcome by key categorical predictors
- If you apply a **transformation** to any variable (e.g., log transformation), explain why, and redo the descriptive statistics for the transformed variable.
- Write a brief narrative summarizing key patterns you observe.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Run Descriptives on the Complete-Case Subsample
Your descriptive statistics must be computed on the **same set of cases** that will be used in the planned model sequence. Create one complete-case analysis dataset for all variables in the largest planned model, then use those same rows for descriptives and every nested model. This ensures consistency between Parts 4 and 5.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## What to Look for in Your Descriptives
- **Skewed distributions**: Does the histogram of your outcome variable look roughly symmetric, or is it heavily skewed? A skewed outcome may benefit from a log transformation.
- **Outliers**: Are there extreme values in scatterplots that could be influential?
- **Expected patterns**: Do the boxplots show group differences that align with your research questions?
- **Adequate variation**: If a categorical predictor has a category with very few observations, your regression estimates for that group may be unstable.
:::


## Part 5: Results --- Regression Analyses {#sec-linear-part5}

*Maximum length: 2 pages of text + up to 2 pages of tables or essential diagnostic figures*

This is the core of your project. You will fit the regression model(s) described in Part 3, check assumptions, and interpret the focal results in substantive terms.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 5
1. **Fit the model(s)** from Part 3 and present a publication-style regression table that includes:
   - Unstandardized coefficients (`b`)
   - Standard errors (SE)
   - 95% confidence intervals
   - *p*-values
   - $N$, $R^2$, adjusted $R^2$, and model-comparison or overall *F*-tests when applicable
2. **Check for collinearity**: Report VIF values using a tool such as `performance::check_collinearity()`. As a convention, flag VIF values above 5 for discussion and treat values above 10 as more serious; thresholds are rules of thumb, not automatic decision rules.
3. **Check linearity and spread**: Use scatterplots, residuals-vs-fitted plots, LOWESS/smooth trends, or the `performance::check_model()` dashboard. If plots suggest curvature, compare a theoretically justified transformation or polynomial term and explain the choice. If unequal spread is a concern, HC3 robust standard errors are an appropriate sensitivity check or reporting option; they address unequal residual variance in ordinary regression, not nonlinearity, omitted variables, clustering, or survey-design issues.
4. **Check residual distribution**: Use a Q-Q plot or residual histogram to assess whether the normality assumption is reasonable for inference.
5. **Check influential cases**: Examine influence diagnostics such as Cook's distance. A cutoff such as $4/n$ is a sensitive screening rule, especially in larger samples; flagged cases should be inspected and discussed, not automatically deleted.
6. **Check independence/design issues**: Briefly note whether the study design creates clustering or repeated observations. If it does, discuss the limitation or use an appropriate remedy.
7. **Categorical predictors with 3+ categories**: Report a term-level or planned nested-model *F*-test for the set of dummy variables, in addition to the individual coefficient tests. Remember that ordinary `anova()` output is sequential unless you are using it for a planned nested-model comparison.
8. **Interpret coefficients substantively**: Interpret every non-intercept coefficient in the primary/final model. For earlier nested models, summarize model fit changes and key coefficient changes. Interpret the intercept only if it has a meaningful baseline interpretation.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to Interpret Coefficients
For each predictor, write a sentence like:

- **Continuous predictor**: "A one-unit increase in [X] is associated with a [b] unit increase (or decrease) in [Y], holding all other variables constant (`b = value`, `p = value`)."
- **Dummy variable**: "On average, [group] scored [b] units higher (or lower) on [Y] than [reference group], holding all other variables constant ($b = \text{value}$, $p = \text{value}$)."

Always include the direction, the magnitude, and a reference to statistical significance.

For a logged outcome, the exact percent change for coefficient $b$ is
$100 \times [\exp(b) - 1]$. The shortcut $100 \times b$ is only an
approximation and is best for small coefficients.

Standardized coefficients may be useful when comparing continuous predictors measured on very different scales, but your main interpretation should use the unstandardized coefficient in the original outcome units.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## If You Include Interactions
Only include an interaction when it answers a specific research question. Test the interaction block before focusing on individual interaction coefficients, and interpret the results as **conditional associations**. Once an interaction is in the model, main effects are no longer unconditional "overall" effects; they are effects at the reference value or zero value of the interacting variable. A simple predicted-value plot or conditional-effect table is often the clearest way to explain the finding.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Regression Table Format
A well-organized regression table should be created with a reproducible table function such as `gtsummary::tbl_regression(model, intercept = TRUE)`. It might look like this:

| Predictor | `b` | SE | 95% CI | *p* |
|-----------|---------|------|--------|-------|
| (Intercept) | 1250.3 | 120.5 | [1013.1, 1487.5] | < .001 |
| Education (years) | 85.2 | 12.3 | [61.1, 109.3] | < .001 |
| Gender: Female | -210.4 | 45.6 | [-299.8, -121.0] | < .001 |
| Region: Suburban | -50.1 | 55.2 | [-158.3, 58.1] | .364 |
| Region: Rural | -130.7 | 60.8 | [-250.0, -11.4] | .032 |

Include $R^2$, adjusted $R^2$, $N$, and the overall or model-comparison *F*-test at the bottom of the table or in a brief note below it.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 5
- **Skipping assumption checks.** You must address collinearity (VIF), linearity, and residual diagnostics. Even if everything looks fine, state that you checked and briefly describe what you found.
- **Reporting only *p*-values without interpretation.** Saying "education is significant at *p* < .001" is not enough. You must interpret the coefficient in context.
- **Ignoring non-significant predictors.** Every focal predictor and control in your final model should be interpreted concisely, whether it is statistically significant or not.
- **Forgetting the overall *F*-test for multi-category predictors.** If `region` has three categories (two dummies), the individual *p*-values do not tell you whether the variable as a whole is significant. Report the *F*-test.
- **Treating diagnostics as a box-checking exercise.** Diagnostics should lead to a short conclusion about whether the model is adequate or whether a limitation/remedy should be discussed.
:::


## Part 6: Discussion {#sec-linear-part6}

*Maximum length: half a page*

The discussion ties everything together. Step back from the numbers and address what the results mean in the context of your research questions.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 6
- **Answer each research question** by referring to the relevant regression results from Part 5.
- Discuss the **practical significance** of your findings --- are the estimated associations or group differences meaningful in the real world?
- Note any **limitations** of your analysis (e.g., cross-sectional data cannot establish causation, potential confounders not included).
- Be **concise**. Do not repeat the numbers from Part 5. Instead, focus on the meaning and implications.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 6
- **Restating regression coefficients verbatim.** The discussion should focus on what the results *mean*, not re-list the numbers.
- **Overclaiming causation.** Unless your study design supports causal inference, use language like "is associated with" rather than "causes" or "affects."
- **Ignoring non-significant findings.** A non-significant result is still a finding. Briefly discuss what it might mean.
:::


## Part 7: Appendix and Reproducibility Materials {#sec-linear-part7}

The appendix contains supporting materials that are important but would
interrupt the flow of the main text. Your source file is submitted with the
report as reproducibility evidence.
For diagnostic placement, clean-session checks, and troubleshooting templates,
see @sec-diagnostics-reproducibility-templates.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 7
- **Residual diagnostic graphs**: Include at minimum a residuals-vs-fitted-values plot and a Q-Q plot of residuals.
- **Supporting output**: Include bulky diagnostics, influence checks, or sensitivity checks that are important but too detailed for the main text.
- **Annotated source file**: Submit your complete `.qmd` or `.R` source file,
  organized and commented so that a reader could reproduce your analysis. It
  should run from start to finish without errors in a clean R session.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Tips for the Source File
- Use **comments** (`#`) generously to explain each step.
- Organize the file in the same order as the project: package loading, data loading, data cleaning, complete-case dataset, descriptives, regression, diagnostics, tables, and figures.
- Make sure the source runs **from a clean R session** without relying on objects left in your workspace.
- Use relative paths or `here::here()` rather than absolute paths tied to your own computer.
- Include the code that creates your tables and figures; do not rely on pasted screenshots or manually edited output.
- Include any data transformations (e.g., log transformations, recoding) so the reader can see exactly what you did.
:::


## Submission Checklist {#sec-linear-checklist}

Before you submit Project 1, use the checklist below as the guide's final completeness check. Worked-example checklists show how the examples apply these expectations, but this checklist is the one to use for your own submission.

::: {.callout-important}
## Project 1 Submission Checklist

**Part 1 --- Background and Research Questions**

- [ ] Topic is motivated with a clear rationale
- [ ] 2--3 research questions are stated and numbered (RQ1, RQ2, ...)
- [ ] No data descriptions appear in Part 1

**Part 2 --- Methods: Subjects and Variables**

- [ ] Data source and study design are described
- [ ] Sample is described (who, how many)
- [ ] Every variable lists its name, type, and scale
- [ ] Every categorical variable has a defined reference category
- [ ] Dummy variables are explicitly defined
- [ ] Starting N, exclusions/restrictions, missingness, and final analytic N are reported

**Part 3 --- Methods: Statistical Analysis**

- [ ] Planned model sequence is stated
- [ ] Each model is linked to specific research questions
- [ ] All nested models use the same complete-case analysis dataset
- [ ] Significance level ($\alpha$) is stated

**Part 4 --- Results: Descriptive Analyses**

- [ ] Sample size and missing data are reported
- [ ] Means, SDs, minimums, and maximums for continuous variables
- [ ] Frequencies for categorical variables
- [ ] Histogram of outcome variable is included
- [ ] Scatterplots (outcome vs. continuous predictors) are included
- [ ] Boxplots (outcome by categorical predictors) are included
- [ ] Descriptives are based on the complete-case subsample
- [ ] Any transformations are described and descriptives are updated

**Part 5 --- Results: Regression Analyses**

- [ ] Publication-style regression table with `b`, SE, CI, *p*-values, $N$, $R^2$, and adjusted $R^2$
- [ ] VIF values are reported (collinearity check)
- [ ] Linearity/spread is assessed with plots or `check_model()`
- [ ] Residual diagnostics are summarized (detailed graphs in Appendix)
- [ ] Influence/Cook's distance and independence/design issues are addressed
- [ ] Overall *F*-test reported for categorical predictors with 3+ categories
- [ ] Final-model non-intercept coefficients are interpreted substantively

**Part 6 --- Discussion**

- [ ] Each research question is addressed
- [ ] Results are discussed in context (not just numbers repeated)
- [ ] Limitations are acknowledged

**Part 7 --- Appendix and Reproducibility Materials**

- [ ] Residual diagnostic graphs are included
- [ ] Annotated `.qmd` or `.R` source file is included and runs without errors
:::
